speaker: interviewer
text: --is Alex Davis. It's March the 11th, 1996. Now, after the Americans dropped the leaflets--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the SS scooped it up, but we managed to save a few and it was circulated in the various barracks. And needless to say, it gave us some encouragement, because it gave us an idea how close the Allies are towards us. However, the situation became more-- more adverse, because the population of the camp started to increase very rapidly. They started to bring in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people from other camps. And while this camp originally held about 20,000, 25,000 people, it rapidly increased to 40,000 to 50,000 to 60,000, to as high as the rumor was that there are 120,000 people now in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a heavy, heavy load. They couldn't feed us. So that little bit of coffee that they gave us in the-- in the morning, around 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock after-- after it depends when the appell was finished, that either didn't come at all, or it came in-- in the afternoon. And similarly with the turnip soup, either it-- it didn't come anymore at 1 o'clock. It came either 4 o'clock in the afternoon or 6 o'clock at night or 12 o'clock midnight, and-- or it came the following morning at 4 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the famine was so huge that I remember those faces that, instead of going to lie down and-- and just stretch out, they would be leaning against the pillars of the-- wooden pillars of the-- of the barrack with that little can or that little dish that they may have had, waiting for that little bit of soup to come in. And incidentally that little can or that little dish that they had, if they lost it or somebody stole it from 'em, then that was the finish. And similarly if somebody got dysentery, that was also the finale. That was it. And people were dying on daily basis. And it-- it was no longer-- you know, you didn't even pay too much attention. You just piled them up like a stack of wood. And then the wagon that would come and pile them up, you know, just throw them out and take them into the crematorium. That was it. It was a common sight that happened twice daily, so it wasn't frightening. Now, we were adjacent to the stalag that contained the Dutch Jews. And they were-- they were treated similarly to us. They also had their own clothes on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- [SIGHS] in March-- from-- in the last three weeks of March of 1944-- 1945, pardon me. 1945 was most crucial, because people were dropping dead by the-- by the lot, you know. They were dying and-- and you saw the wagon hauling away the corpses, you know, in-- in a more frequent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] period. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Towards the end of March, I believe it was around March 27, 28, it was Pesach, I remember that, when we-- all of a sudden we were ordered that the younger people have to report to work to go and clean out some cisterns that were located near the kitchens, because they-- during the winter, they gathered an awful lot of grime and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we were given the orders to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to pump out with the water from those cisterns and to clean out those basins. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So 40 people were selected from our barrack. And we were taken out. It was the first seder night, was supposed to be the first seder night. We were taken out, it was a horrible rain and cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a hand pump that we had like 10 people on either side that we were pumping out. And there were tons of water in that-- in that cistern. And then every so often, every half an hour, they would change and then the other 20 would come in. And no food, no nothing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were the kapos that were with us. And I remember there was that Greek Jewish kapo that was over us. And he was just commanding us how to pump the water, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2. And this was going on all night. We were-- we were practically dropping off our-- of our feet. We were adjacent to the kitchen. And we were searching-- while we were relieved from one shift to the other, we were searching for some food. And I found some-- in one corner I found some turnips and some onions, rotten onions it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So obviously we grabbed at anything that we could stuff in our mouth. But we didn't realize that the guards from the tower with their search lights-- they were going on constantly-- that they saw us doing that. And when they were relieved at midnight, they-- he came down and he started to yell and he cocked up his rifle. And he says, you guys were stealing, you know. We saw you from the-- What were we stealing? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he cocked up his rifle. And I was the lucky one that he picked out, ready to shoot. But he didn't shoot, because this huge kapo, this Greek kapo came and he started to slap me around and he knocked me to the ground. And that sort of calmed down the German. And-- and he let me go. And anyway, we pumped that water 'til 6 o'clock in the morning, at which time we were relieved and hauled back into the barrack. And the following afternoon we were hauled out again to another cistern to do the same thing. And at that time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, I-- we were again next to a kitchen where were heaps of turnips were there. And people became [INAUDIBLE]. In this they-- they tied up their pants, and they were dropping the turnips into their pants. [SIGHS] I was a little bit smarter at that time and I didn't do it. And lo and behold again, the-- and from the tower, the guard saw that. And when he was relieved, he came down and he started to make a ruckus that we were stealing turnips. By-- by that time-- put a pillow over it-- by that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the officers, the oberscharfuhrer and all the SS came and they started to haul in the-- round up the guys. And they got a terrific beating, you know. The kapos came, the Ukrainians, and they pulled them over a barrel, you know. And they were with huge, huge sticks. And they were beating them. As a matter of fact, two dies-- two guys died there. They knocked off their-- their kidneys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was towards the end of March. And April came in and the-- the-- the weather got a little bit milder. And we were given the-- the task to clean out all the trenches and the-- and-- and the gutters, you know, that were also filled with all kinds of debris. And at that time we came closer to a camp-- to a stalag that contained some of the boys with the striped pajamas. That was immediately after the Dutch stalag. And lo and behold, as I was cleaning the gutter, we were talking to them through the fence. And quite a number of my schoolmates that I was going to school with them from back home, from my hometown, they were in that stalag. And I didn't-- they were beyond recognition. They were already so emaciated that they would be-- I just couldn't recognize them. In any event, to this went on 'til around April the 7th or the 8th, when all of a sudden we got word that our stalag will be evacuated. And we were divided in two groups. One group was to leave one day. And the next group was to leave the other day, the following day. And we were marched out from the camp. And we didn't know where we are going, but we were told that we are going. That's all. And we were again put into freight cars. And little did we know by that time that the British army was only about 8 to 10 kilometers away from us. And we were taken out. We were evacuated. But the way we went to the train-- to the railway station, there were columns of hundreds and hundreds of hundreds emaciated people coming, you know, that could hardly walk, that they were dragging themselves, urged on by the SS with-- with all kinds of beatings. And we were going on one side of the road. They were on the other side of the road. In any event, we were put into the wagons. And we were taken-- the-- the train pulled out. And we were traveling we didn't know where. We didn't know nothing. By that time, the SS that were escorting us were a little bit on the milder side. They were-- they were in a separate wagon, and they let us get out of our wagons to go out. And as a matter of fact, we came in to a city called Luneburg, something, Luneburg. And it was immediately after that particular railway station was heavily bombarded by the Allies. And the freight cars were filled with food, with cigarettes, with chocolates, with everything. And we sort of let loose there and we raided that place. And we tried to stuff ourselves with whatever we could. And we were crawling from one place to another. I was just crawling under-- under one of the freight cars to go into another freight car when one of the Sonderwaffen-- what they called it-- these were in the black uniforms with a-- shoved a pistol against me. He says-- and he didn't shoot, but he yelled in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- this is enough, get back, which we did. We were traveling for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] several days, because the train apparently was zig-zagging, trying to get through the Elbe River. She couldn't get through the Elbe River, because they were cut off, 'til finally they found one place where we-- they-- they got through. And after several days of travel, we finally came into Theresienstadt. In Theresienstadt, we were thrown into a pit that apparently was an old castle. And this was the back part of a castle with a tunnel underneath. And it was a very, very rainy day, I remember. And we were kept there for a couple of days. During the night, we sort of managed to smuggle into that tunnel, to try to be shielded from-- from the rain. And we were-- about two days later, we were marched out from there. And we were taken into one of the armories, because Theresienstadt was composed of many armories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taken into the-- what was it called now, the name of the city? The Dresdner Kaserne, I think it was. And we were billeted in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now this was already solid buildings-- it wasn't barracks. Solid buildings. And I was-- for our group, I was given the task to haul coal from the basement up to the second floor where the kitchen was situated. And there it was regularly given already soup every-- every day. And we had to haul that coal back and forth. And then, at night, we were given the task to clean up the kitchen. We had a little bit of privilege. We got a little bit of extra soup for that work. But we knew that typhoid was on the breakout. We heard already isolated cases. Every day you heard more and more that typhoid is breaking out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was going on 'til [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May, May 9th. On May 9th, in the evening, we were doing our usual work cleaning up the kitchen around 9:00, 9:30 in the evening, when my fellow worker, whom I refer to as a German Jew, young, younger man, we were cleaning up the kitchen. He was looking down from the second floor window. And he saw some people in strange uniforms. And he told me in German, he says, oh, he says, they must be some French prisoners of war. We didn't pay too much attention, until about 50 minutes later, a Russian tank moved through. And then the Russian army started to move in. And we knew already there was a whole, whole upheaval in the camp. Everybody's dancing and jumping, you know, the Russians came in. With us knowing that-- the way typhoid was breaking out-- we were determined the following morning to leave camp and to go towards Prague, which we did. We walked for about six to eight kilometers. We had to turn back, because the Germans were still fighting on and there was still heavy activity going on. So we went back to the-- to the camp-- to the Dresdner Kaserne. And we waited 'til the following morning. The following morning around 4:00 o'clock in the morning, we got up and we started to walk towards Prague. And we walked and we walked and we walked all day long, 'til around noontime we caught onto a Russian column, a horse-- a horse-drawn wagon. And we begged them that they should take us on, which they did. And they took us on. And we traveled with them, until we came to a village in Czechoslovakia. We didn't know where we were, but we traveled with them. And they-- they drove into a farmhouse. And there they organized themselves. And they cooked themselves a goulash there, with cabbage soup. And that was the first time that they gave us some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Early in the morning, the following morning, they got up. And we went with them for a little while. And then they said they are turning off this road, and if we want to go to Prague, we have to go straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to walk. And we walked. And we came into Saturday. Around Saturday noon, we came into a rather populated place. And we didn't know where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- although I spoke German reasonably well, I was afraid to speak German because, you know, the atmosphere was very anti-German at that time. And I tried to-- I saw this man in uniform, very beautiful uniform, and I tried to talk to him and tried to talk to him in Hungarian and English, Romanian. And he didn't know any of the languages, 'til finally I told him, maybe in German, a little-- I forgot that I was in Sudetenland, which was-- had a great German population. He says, yeah, yeah. Why didn't you speak German, he says. So we told him that we come from Theresienstadt-- Terezin, there we call it Terezin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when he heard the word Terezin, he says, you are coming from Terezin. He says, you come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took us to a place [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that was a Blue Cross place. And when they heard that we came from Terezin, they took us around. And they-- what can they do for us? And they gave us clothes. And they gave us food. And-- and they immediately assigned a young scout that he should be our escort.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this scout spoke a reasonably good English. And we conversed in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he told us-- he took us wherever we want to go. And he want to know what happened in Terezin, because he knew it was a concentration camp and everything. And he-- they just didn't know what to do for us. In any event, he said that we are going to be billeted in a monastery, which we were. But by late evening, the-- there were thousands of people flocking in, because there were two camps, actually. One was Theresienstadt that contained quite a huge number of people. And then there was Litomerice that was about 12, 14, kilometers away. And all those converged on Prague. And to put them up, they were mostly sent to the-- to the monastery, or to similar places. [SIGHS] So we slept through the night. And then the following morning this-- this scout-- his name I'll never forget in my life. His name was Yaroslav Lavtser. And he was our guide. And he took us around. And he took us again to the Red Cross place. And this was Sunday. And Sunday, we walked around in Prague to get acquainted there. And we were to report back on Monday noon to the Red Cross, at which time the Red Cross gave us tickets, some money, some food. And they told us to go to this and this railway station. We told them that we want to go to Budapest. And again, we got onto these freight cars. And after a lengthy duration-- it took us about a week-- we arrived in Budapest. In Budapest, there was already a well organized community that was called the [INAUDIBLE], an offshoot of the JDC. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were taken into a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a place where we-- again, we got fresh clothes and disinfected. And at that time, I told them I want to go to Satu Mare. I want to get home. I didn't know whether I-- who-- who was, or whether anybody's alive, or whatever. Anyhow, I got-- I did arrive in Budapest after about a week. And I went to my old place where I lived. And I remember I got organized and-- we didn't need-- you didn't need any train tickets. If you had some documentation that you are-- you are from a concentration camp, you could travel for free on the train. Well, I got on a train, I met quite a number of people from my home town. And-- but then I got sick on the way. My-- my feet all of a sudden swell up, you know, for one reason or another. So I was taken to the hospital, where I spent several days. And there I found out that my parents are no longer there. They're not-- and we already found out about what happened in Auschwitz. And we heard about-- about them being perished at Auschwitz. And the only thing that I found out, that I had that one sister that went into hiding. And she apparently got married in-- in Halmeu, where I was born. And the fellow that I spoke to, whom I met at a Jewish hospital, told me that he'll let them know within a short time that-- that I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, we got united with my sister. But I couldn't find my place. I had no home. I had no house. I had no parents. I had-- we were drifting from one place to another. So I was traveling-- Were you traveling alone or with someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone. Well, you always found friends. You always found a number of former concentration camp inmates. You know that you connected with them. We had something in common with to talk-- to talk about. I was going to Budapest. From Budapest, I went to Bucharest. And in Bucharest, I had a cousin. And with having a mind that maybe I'll learn a trade there-- he was a furrier-- and I'll stay with him. But when I got to Bucharest, they were more or less under Russian occupation or domination, call it whatever. And somehow, even though I was only 23-years old, I just didn't-- I didn't think that there was any future there. And I decided at that time that I want to emigrate to Palestine, Israel. I had a sister living in Israel. And I got connected with a little Zionist group that were-- actually it was called the Berihah, which means-- tell me what it means, Berihah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The getaway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not the getaway, but refuge, something similar to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this group, you went with this group.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to find sanctuary. Yeah. They organized groups to take them into Austria, eventually to Italy, and then to Israel. I got connected with them. And we went-- I had to go to Budapest. And it was from Budapest that this group would be leaving. And I was-- we tried once to go through the border to Austria and we got caught. We were turned back. And then we went back to Budapest. And then on the second try, we got a group of about 100 kids. And we found a way to go through a small Austrian village, where you had to use a-- by boat. You had to go through the Danube. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were to get on to the American side. Once I got to the American side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were to go to this place that had a refugee camp. It was called Wels-- W-E-L-L-S. And we were herded into one of those refugee camps. And we were given, you know, the usual, the barracks and everything. But as I started to stroll around, I found that adjacent to that camp there was a-- an American military company billeted. It was the 97th Signal company. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Being able to speak English, you know, I-- I approached the sergeant. And I told him that he should let me work, you know, for some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This after doing some work that they were impressed with, they kept me there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wound up working for the American occupation forces for-- in Wels 'til--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's-- let's hold it right there.
